skill.name: wp-orchestrator
skill.description: Coordinates WordPress project discovery, setup, audits, optimization, packaging, and handoff by guiding Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through structured workflows.
skill.summary: Coordinate WordPress site setup, audits, launch checks, and client handoff workflows.
skill.author: CrazySwami

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found extensive shell, filesystem, network, and environment-file patterns. Review confirms these are mostly WordPress workflow documentation and setup examples, not confirmed malicious code, but the skill has broad operational capability that can affect local and production sites. No prompt injection attempt or credential exfiltration intent was found in the reviewed files.

medium_findings.title: Broad Shell and Tool Orchestration
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash access and repeatedly instructs use of Docker, WP-CLI, Git, Python, Node, zip, rsync, and SSH commands. This is legitimate for WordPress operations, but command execution can modify local projects, containers, or production systems if used without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tools and command examples are explicit. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the concern is operational risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Environment and Hidden File Handling
medium_findings.description: The workflow copies and edits .env files and documents hidden Claude skill directories. Environment files commonly contain database credentials, so users must avoid exposing secrets in prompts or commits.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files clearly reference .env creation and hidden directories. The same workflow adds .env to .gitignore, which reduces but does not remove credential-handling risk.

medium_findings.title: Network Downloads and External Service Calls
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes cloning from GitHub, using curl to download WP-CLI, calling PageSpeed, and testing public WordPress URLs. These are expected setup and audit actions, but they require trust in external endpoints and network access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network destinations are visible and related to WordPress setup or audits. No evidence shows hidden exfiltration, but network execution remains a real supply-chain and privacy risk.

medium_findings.title: Production Sync and Database Operations
medium_findings.description: The workflow includes rsync, SFTP, database export, search-replace, and production import examples. These can overwrite files or data if executed against the wrong target.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The production and database commands are explicit and high impact. They are normal deployment guidance, but require backups and human approval.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The scanner reported weak cryptography blockers, but the reviewed locations contain brand, SEO, versioning, or checklist text rather than cryptographic algorithms or hashing code. These are false positives.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed line ranges do not contain crypto APIs or hash functions. The evidence supports dismissing the blocker classification.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Example Targets
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs appear as GitHub repository links, WordPress test URLs, PageSpeed examples, and local sample domains. They are visible documentation examples, not hidden callbacks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are documented in setup and audit instructions. No evidence found that they receive secrets or background telemetry.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Access With File Modification Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill can guide Bash operations that create, copy, remove, package, and sync project files. This pattern should require user confirmation before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration and file-changing command examples are direct evidence. The risk is contextual and depends on user approval and target paths.

dangerous_patterns.title: WP-CLI Eval Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow documents wp eval calls inside WordPress containers. WP-CLI eval can execute PHP in the site context and should be restricted to trusted code and backups.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wp eval examples are explicit and high impact. They call named demo-content functions, so they are not inherently malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Download and Install Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The installation guide downloads WP-CLI with curl and installs it into a system path. Users should verify source authenticity before running these commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command sequence is clear and operational. It uses an official-looking GitHub raw URL, but remote download execution still carries supply-chain risk.

content.user_title: Coordinate WordPress Site Builds and Audits
content.value_statement: WordPress projects often need discovery, setup, QA, SEO, and launch work across several tools. This skill organizes those workflows into guided phases for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
actual_capabilities: Runs a discovery interview for WordPress project requirements.
Coordinates Docker, WordPress Playground, and standard WordPress setup guidance.
Builds phased todo lists for site setup, SEO, performance, security, and launch.
Guides site audits across SEO, visual QA, performance, and security review tasks.
Provides workflow guidance for theme packaging, GitHub deployment, and WP Pusher updates.
Creates client handoff and admin documentation templates.
limitations: It depends on separate WordPress skills for many detailed checks and scripts.
It assumes local tools such as Docker, WP-CLI, Python, Node, and Git are available.
It includes examples that can change files, databases, and production deployments.
It does not replace human review for credentials, hosting access, or launch approval.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Client Website
use_cases.description: Collect requirements, define pages, choose plugins, and create a phased setup checklist before development starts.

use_cases.title: Audit an Existing WordPress Site
use_cases.description: Coordinate SEO, visual, performance, and security checks, then turn findings into prioritized repair tasks.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Site for Launch
use_cases.description: Review content, forms, analytics, backups, packaging, deployment, and client handoff before publishing.

prompt_templates.title: Start Discovery
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin a new WordPress project with basic requirements gathering.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use wp-orchestrator to start discovery for a new WordPress site. Ask me the required questions and create a first todo list.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up Local Development
prompt_templates.scenario: Prepare a local WordPress development environment.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use wp-orchestrator to guide local WordPress setup with Docker. Confirm prerequisites before any command that changes files.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Site Audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Evaluate an existing WordPress site across several quality areas.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use wp-orchestrator to audit this WordPress site for SEO, visual QA, performance, and security. Summarize risks and next actions.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Launch and Handoff
prompt_templates.scenario: Prepare a finished WordPress site for client delivery.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use wp-orchestrator to prepare launch checks, package the theme, verify deployment steps, and draft client handoff documentation.

output_examples.input: Set up a new WordPress project for a local service business.
output: Discovery questions covering business goals, pages, brand assets, hosting, and handoff needs.
A phased setup checklist for environment, theme, plugins, SEO, QA, security, and launch.
A note to confirm before running file, Docker, or network commands.

output_examples.input: Audit my existing WordPress site before redesign.
output: A coordinated audit plan for SEO, screenshots, performance checks, plugin review, and admin hardening.
Prioritized findings grouped by high, medium, and low effort.
A todo list that separates urgent fixes from redesign recommendations.

output_examples.input: Prepare this custom theme for client launch.
output: A launch checklist for content, forms, analytics, backups, caching, security, and documentation.
Packaging and deployment steps with warnings for production database and file sync actions.

best_practices: Review each generated command before execution, especially commands touching production systems.
Use staging environments for audits, syncs, and deployment testing before changing production.
Keep credentials outside prompts and confirm that .env files are ignored by version control.
anti_patterns: Do not run production database imports without a verified backup and rollback plan.
Do not paste hosting passwords, API keys, or client credentials into prompts.
Do not treat generated launch checklists as a substitute for stakeholder approval.

faq.question: Does this skill build WordPress sites by itself?
faq.answer: It coordinates the workflow and delegates many tasks to other WordPress skills or local tools.

faq.question: Can it run shell commands?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill declares Bash access and includes Docker, WP-CLI, Git, Python, and network command examples.

faq.question: Is it safe for production sites?
faq.answer: Use caution. It includes production sync, database import, deployment, and remote server examples.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it require other skills?
faq.answer: Many workflows reference related skills for Docker, SEO, visual QA, performance, branding, and white labeling.

faq.question: What should I check before using it?
faq.answer: Confirm tool availability, repository paths, environment files, backups, and the target site before executing commands.
